h1Title: Renal Artery Stenosis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Renal artery stenosis (RAS) is a narrowing of one or both arteries supplying the kidneys, reducing renal perfusion pressure and triggering the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system to produce renovascular hypertension and, in bilateral or solitary-kidney disease, ischemic nephropathy. Atherosclerosis accounts for roughly 90% of cases and typically affects men over 55 with established vascular disease; fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) accounts for most remaining cases and predominantly affects women aged 15-50. RAS is responsible for 1-5% of all hypertension in the general population but for 20-40% of cases of treatment-resistant hypertension referred to specialist centers. Revascularization by percutaneous transluminal renal angioplasty with stenting benefits carefully selected patients, but medical therapy is equally effective for most atherosclerotic RAS.

keyStats.prevalence: Hemodynamically significant RAS found in 1-5% of hypertensive patients; 20-40% of referrals for resistant hypertension (ASTRAL/CORAL trial populations)
keyStats.demographics: Atherosclerotic RAS: predominantly men >55 with existing cardiovascular disease. FMD-type RAS: predominantly women aged 15-50.
keyStats.avgAge: Atherosclerotic RAS onset typically 55-75 years; FMD onset typically 20-50 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 6-8% prevalence on autopsy in patients with hypertension; true community prevalence likely 0.5-1% of adults

definition: Renal artery stenosis (ICD-10: I70.1 for atherosclerotic; I77.3 for fibromuscular) is a narrowing of the main renal artery or a major branch by at least 50-60% of luminal diameter, sufficient to create a pressure gradient across the stenosis and activate juxtaglomerular renin release. Two principal etiologies dominate. Atherosclerotic RAS, which accounts for 85-90% of cases, develops at the ostium or proximal third of the renal artery as part of systemic atherosclerotic disease; it is associated with coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, and aortic aneurysm. Fibromuscular dysplasia, accounting for 10-15%, involves non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic arterial wall dysplasia; the medial type (also called perimedial FMD) produces a classic beads-on-a-string appearance on angiography in the mid-to-distal renal artery. The hemodynamic consequence is underperfusion of the ischemic kidney, causing renin-mediated hypertension that is often severe, asymmetric, or refractory to multiple antihypertensive agents. In bilateral disease or a unilateral RAS affecting a solitary functional kidney, the entire GFR depends on the narrowed vessel; ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy can precipitate acute kidney injury by blocking the angiotensin-mediated efferent arteriolar tone that maintains glomerular filtration under these conditions. Nephrology, vascular surgery, and interventional radiology manage RAS collaboratively.

typesClassification.description: Plaque at the ostium or proximal renal artery; part of systemic atherosclerosis. Progresses in 20-40% of cases over 5 years to higher-grade stenosis. Revascularization benefits are limited in most patients (ASTRAL, CORAL trials); medical therapy plus cardiovascular risk reduction is first-line.

Non-inflammatory dysplasia of the medial or intimal arterial wall. Classic beads-on-a-string pattern on angiography. Predominantly affects women under 50. Responds well to percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) without stenting; angioplasty achieves cure or meaningful BP reduction in 40-60% of patients.

Stenosis of both renal arteries or of a unilateral artery supplying a solitary kidney. Causes flash pulmonary edema and ischemic nephropathy. ACE inhibitor or ARB use can precipitate acute kidney injury; bilateral revascularization may rescue renal function if performed before irreversible nephron loss.

Involvement of segmental or accessory arteries; more common in FMD. May cause segmental renal ischemia with focally elevated renin production. Diagnosis requires selective renal artery angiography or CT angiography with thin-slice reconstruction.

primarySymptoms: Hypertension that is severe, sudden-onset after age 55, or refractory to three antihypertensive medications including a diuretic — the classic presentation of renovascular hypertension.

Acute kidney injury after starting an ACE inhibitor or ARB in a patient with bilateral RAS or solitary kidney disease, as these drugs remove angiotensin-mediated efferent arteriolar tone that maintains GFR.

Recurrent flash pulmonary edema (Pickering syndrome) — acute pulmonary congestion without severely impaired systolic function — caused by bilateral RAS-driven activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
Worsening chronic kidney disease unexplained by diabetes, glomerulonephritis, or other primary renal disease in a patient with extensive cardiovascular disease.
A renal bruit heard on auscultation over one or both flanks, present in 40-50% of hemodynamically significant lesions but with low specificity.
Asymmetric kidney sizes on ultrasound (difference in kidney length exceeding 1.5 cm) suggesting chronic underperfusion on the stenosed side.
Hypokalemia from secondary hyperaldosteronism — the renin-aldosterone cascade promotes renal potassium wasting in severe renovascular disease.
Episodic headache and malignant hypertension with fundoscopic changes (flame hemorrhages, papilledema) in young women with FMD.
earlyWarningSigns: New hypertension in a patient under 30, particularly a young woman, warrants FMD screening with CT or MR angiography.
Abrupt worsening of previously well-controlled hypertension after age 55 in a smoker with known cardiovascular disease.
Creatinine rise exceeding 30% within one week of starting an ACE inhibitor or ARB without other explanation.
Unexplained asymmetry in kidney length exceeding 1.5 cm found incidentally on abdominal ultrasound.
Hypokalemia on routine biochemistry in a hypertensive patient not on diuretics, suggesting secondary aldosteronism.
emergencySigns: Acute pulmonary edema in a patient with near-normal left ventricular function — suggests bilateral RAS (Pickering syndrome) requiring urgent renal imaging and specialist review.
Accelerated malignant hypertension with fundoscopic changes, neurological symptoms, or serum creatinine doubling within days.
Acute kidney injury after ACE inhibitor or ARB initiation with serum creatinine rise above 30% — hold the drug immediately and evaluate for bilateral or high-grade unilateral RAS.
Hypertensive emergency in a young woman with severe headache — FMD-related RAS can cause malignant hypertension and concurrent FMD of the carotid or vertebral arteries with dissection.

causes.cause: Atherosclerotic plaque at the renal artery ostium
causes.description: Atherosclerotic plaque originating from the aortic wall extends into the ostium and proximal renal artery, typically as a continuation of aortic disease. The resulting stenosis reduces perfusion pressure and stimulates renin release from juxtaglomerular cells. Risk factors mirror systemic atherosclerosis: age, smoking, diabetes, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease.

causes.cause: Fibromuscular dysplasia of the medial or intimal arterial wall
causes.description: FMD is a non-atherosclerotic, non-inflammatory arteriopathy affecting the medial layer of small and medium arteries. The renal arteries and carotid/vertebral arteries are most commonly affected. The etiology involves mechanical stress, hormonal factors (explaining female predominance), and genetic predisposition. Medial FMD creates a classic string-of-beads pattern from alternating areas of fibroplasia and aneurysm formation.

A large-vessel granulomatous vasculitis predominantly affecting women under 40. Involvement of the renal arteries occurs in 30-40% of cases, causing stenosis or occlusion. Renal FDG-PET and CTA demonstrate inflammatory thickening. Immunosuppressive therapy (steroids, methotrexate) is the primary treatment; revascularization is planned only during disease remission.

Retroperitoneal fibrosis, lymph node masses, or congenital aortic anomalies can extrinsically compress the renal arteries. Coarctation of the abdominal aorta may reduce perfusion to both renal arteries simultaneously, causing severe renovascular hypertension in children and young adults.

Radiotherapy targeting retroperitoneal or pelvic malignancies can cause accelerated fibrosis and stenosis of the renal arteries years after treatment, presenting as new hypertension or declining GFR in a cancer survivor.

riskFactors.description: Atherosclerotic RAS prevalence rises sharply with age, reaching 7-10% in patients over 70 with established cardiovascular disease. Men are disproportionately affected in the atherosclerotic form.

Current smoking nearly doubles the risk of atherosclerotic RAS and is associated with more rapid progression of stenosis. Smoking cessation reduces the rate of contralateral kidney disease progression.

Diabetes accelerates aorto-ostial atherosclerosis and increases the probability of bilateral renal artery involvement. Diabetic nephropathy and atherosclerotic RAS often coexist, complicating diagnostic workup.

Elevated LDL-cholesterol and low HDL-cholesterol drive atherosclerotic plaque development at the renal artery ostium. Statin therapy reduces plaque progression and cardiovascular events but has not been shown to reverse established RAS.

riskFactors.factor: Established cardiovascular or peripheral vascular disease
riskFactors.description: Patients with coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, or aortic aneurysm have a 10-40% prevalence of concurrent hemodynamically significant RAS. All three vascular beds should be assessed when new hypertension or declining renal function occurs.
FMD affects women in up to 90% of cases, with peak onset between 20 and 50 years. Hormonal factors, connective tissue laxity, and mechanical arterial wall stress are proposed mechanisms.

affectedDemographics: Atherosclerotic RAS: predominantly white and Black men aged 55-80 with established cardiovascular disease; the ASTRAL trial enrolled a mean age of 66 years.
FMD-type RAS: 90% of cases in women; median age at diagnosis 50 years but disease begins in the 20s-30s; occurs across all ethnicities.
Bilateral RAS: associated with older age, more advanced atherosclerosis, and shorter time to ESRD compared with unilateral disease.
Pediatric RAS: rare; most often due to neurofibromatosis type 1, aortic coarctation, or mid-aortic syndrome. Presents with severe hypertension and failure to thrive.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnostic pathway for RAS begins with clinical suspicion: renovascular hypertension should be considered in any patient with resistant hypertension, sudden-onset hypertension over age 55, recurrent flash pulmonary edema, or a rise in creatinine after ACE inhibitor or ARB initiation. The American Heart Association and the European Society of Hypertension recommend non-invasive imaging as the initial step. Renal duplex Doppler ultrasound is the first-line test in most centers, assessing the main renal arteries for peak systolic velocity exceeding 200 cm/s (stenosis >60%) and a resistive index in the parenchyma. Limitations include operator dependence and difficult visualization in obese patients. CT angiography (CTA) with three-dimensional reconstruction provides excellent anatomical detail of the renal arteries, aorta, and accessory vessels, and is the preferred modality when duplex ultrasound is non-diagnostic, though nephrotoxic contrast must be weighed against baseline GFR. MR angiography (MRA) avoids iodinated contrast and radiation and is preferred when eGFR exceeds 30 mL/min/1.73 m². Gadolinium-based contrast agents are contraindicated below eGFR 30 due to nephrogenic systemic fibrosis risk; unenhanced MRA techniques are less sensitive. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) remains the gold standard for anatomy and hemodynamic assessment and is performed at the same sitting as planned renal artery intervention. Captopril renal scintigraphy (using ACE inhibitor provocation with nuclear medicine) was historically used but has low sensitivity and is rarely performed today. Renal vein renin sampling demonstrating a ratio exceeding 1.5 between the affected and contralateral kidney indicates functional significance of the stenosis but is technically demanding and uncommonly performed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line non-invasive screening for main renal artery stenosis; assesses peak systolic velocity and parenchymal resistive index
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound gel applied to the abdomen. The scan takes 30-60 minutes, longer if the patient is obese. No radiation or contrast. A peak systolic velocity above 200 cm/s in the main renal artery suggests stenosis exceeding 60%. Sensitivity 85%, specificity 92% in expert hands.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High-resolution anatomical assessment of renal artery stenosis, aortic plaque, and accessory vessels
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IV iodinated contrast is injected via an arm cannula during the scan. Takes 5-10 minutes. Provides detailed images of the renal arteries, aorta, and branch vessels. Creatinine must be checked beforehand; NSAIDs and metformin are held for 48 hours around the procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Contrast-enhanced or unenhanced arterial imaging; preferred when CTA contrast is contraindicated
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the MRI scanner. Gadolinium contrast is used if eGFR exceeds 30 mL/min/1.73 m². Avoids radiation. Sensitivity comparable to CTA for main renal artery stenosis; less reliable for accessory branches.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard anatomical and hemodynamic assessment; performed when revascularization is planned
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Invasive procedure under local anaesthesia or sedation. A catheter is inserted via the femoral or radial artery to the renal artery orifice; contrast is injected. Simultaneous measurement of trans-lesional pressure gradient. Recovery 4-6 hours. Carries 1-2% risk of access-site complications.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated renin with high aldosterone supports a renin-mediated mechanism; useful when results are very high or for differentiating primary aldosteronism
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draws in the morning after 30 minutes upright; patient should not take ACE inhibitors or diuretics for 2 weeks beforehand. Results within 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum creatinine and eGFR before and after ACE inhibitor/ARB initiation
diagnosticTests.purpose: A rise in creatinine exceeding 30% within one week of starting ACE inhibitor or ARB is a functional indicator of hemodynamically significant bilateral or high-grade unilateral RAS
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw at 5-7 days after starting the medication. Creatinine rise of up to 20-25% is acceptable with careful monitoring; above 30% warrants stopping the drug and imaging.

treatmentOverview: Management of RAS requires distinguishing patients most likely to benefit from revascularization from those who will do equally well — or better — on optimized medical therapy. Two landmark randomized trials — ASTRAL (2009) and CORAL (2014) — demonstrated that percutaneous renal artery stenting plus medical therapy does not improve cardiovascular outcomes, renal function, or blood pressure compared with optimal medical therapy alone in patients with atherosclerotic RAS. On this basis, current ESC/ESH 2023 and AHA/ACC guidelines restrict revascularization to specific indications: recurrent flash pulmonary edema attributable to bilateral RAS, rapid renal function deterioration in bilateral RAS, and hemodynamically confirmed severe unilateral stenosis with rescue of a solitary kidney. For FMD-type RAS, the evidence base supports percutaneous transluminal angioplasty without stenting as first-line revascularization, achieving cure or meaningful blood pressure reduction in 40-65% of patients. Medical therapy for atherosclerotic RAS includes ACE inhibitors or ARBs (used carefully with creatinine monitoring in bilateral disease), statins to reduce cardiovascular events, antiplatelet therapy, and tight control of blood pressure, blood glucose, and smoking status. In bilateral RAS where ACE inhibitors are used, serum creatinine monitoring at 5-7 days is mandatory. Beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers are effective as additional antihypertensives. When ischemic nephropathy has caused significant nephron loss, the response to revascularization diminishes; fibrosis-predominant CKD is irreversible. Renal replacement therapy is the endpoint for patients who reach ESRD from ischemic nephropathy.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors or ARBs (e.g., ramipril 5-10 mg/day; irbesartan 150-300 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for renovascular hypertension in unilateral RAS. Block the angiotensin-mediated hypertensive drive. Contraindicated in bilateral RAS or solitary kidney RAS without close creatinine monitoring. Check creatinine and potassium at 5-7 days after initiation; a rise exceeding 30% warrants stopping.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Blood pressure reduction achieved in 85-90% of unilateral RAS; may precipitate acute kidney injury in bilateral disease — use with caution and monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: High-intensity statins (atorvastatin 40-80 mg/day; rosuvastatin 20-40 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for cardiovascular risk reduction in all patients with atherosclerotic RAS, regardless of LDL level. Reduce plaque progression and major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) in patients with coexisting coronary or peripheral artery disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 40-50% relative risk reduction in MACE in patients with atherosclerotic vascular disease (multiple statin trials); no trials specifically in RAS alone

medicalTreatments.name: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75-100 mg/day or clopidogrel 75 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended for secondary cardiovascular prevention in atherosclerotic RAS. Reduces thromboembolic events including renal artery thrombosis at the site of severe stenosis or after stenting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dual antiplatelet therapy for 3-6 months after renal artery stenting is standard practice; aspirin alone for ongoing medical management of atherosclerotic disease

medicalTreatments.name: Amlodipine (5-10 mg/day) or other calcium channel blockers
medicalTreatments.description: Effective antihypertensives in RAS when ACE inhibitors or ARBs are not tolerated or are contraindicated (bilateral disease). Do not affect renal artery stenosis hemodynamics but control blood pressure through arterial vasodilation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Blood pressure control rates comparable to other antihypertensives; no intrinsic renoprotective benefit specific to RAS

medicalTreatments.name: Smoking cessation
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory in atherosclerotic RAS. Smoking accelerates plaque progression, increases restenosis rate after stenting, and raises risk of cardiovascular death 2-3 fold. Cessation interventions include varenicline, bupropion, nicotine replacement, and behavioral counseling.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Smoking cessation reduces the rate of contralateral renal artery disease progression and overall cardiovascular mortality by 30-40%

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous transluminal renal angioplasty (PTRA) with stenting for atherosclerotic RAS
surgicalOptions.description: Balloon dilation of the stenosed renal artery followed by placement of a bare-metal stent. Performed under local anesthesia via femoral or radial artery access. ASTRAL and CORAL trials showed no benefit over medical therapy for routine atherosclerotic RAS; reserved for specific high-risk presentations (flash pulmonary edema, bilateral RAS with declining GFR).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success rate 95%; primary patency at 1 year approximately 80%; restenosis rate 15-25% at 2 years in atherosclerotic lesions

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) without stenting for FMD
surgicalOptions.description: Balloon dilation alone is preferred for FMD because the non-calcified media responds well to angioplasty. Stenting is reserved for angioplasty failure or dissection. FMD-related RAS responds markedly better to revascularization than atherosclerotic RAS.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of hypertension (no medications needed) in 20-40% of FMD patients; meaningful BP reduction in an additional 40-50%; clinical benefit maintained in 70-80% at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Open surgical renal revascularization (aortorenal bypass)
surgicalOptions.description: Saphenous vein or synthetic graft bypass from the aorta to the post-stenotic renal artery. Reserved for complex lesions involving the aorta, multiple renal arteries, or pediatric cases where stenting is unsuitable. Carries higher perioperative risk than endovascular approaches.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term patency of 70-85% at 5 years; suitable for young FMD patients or aortic disease requiring concurrent repair

alternativeTreatments.name: Renal denervation (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Catheter-based radiofrequency ablation of renal sympathetic nerves to treat resistant hypertension. RDN is not approved specifically for RAS and would not address the structural stenosis; investigational only.

whySeeSpecialist: A nephrologist or vascular medicine specialist should evaluate any patient with suspected RAS to distinguish it from primary hypertension and to coordinate imaging and treatment decisions. Interventional radiology or vascular surgery involvement is required if revascularization is planned. Cardiology co-management is often needed given the high rate of concurrent coronary artery disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a center with a dedicated renovascular hypertension service capable of CT angiography, MR angiography, and duplex ultrasound — and that can offer both endovascular and surgical revascularization. For FMD, centers with high FMD case volume achieve better outcomes with angioplasty. The FMD Society of America maintains a list of specialist centers.
hospitalCriteria: Non-invasive vascular imaging including CT angiography and MR angiography
Renal duplex Doppler ultrasound performed by experienced vascular technologists
Interventional radiology capable of renal artery angioplasty and stenting
Vascular surgery for open renal revascularization when endovascular approach is unsuitable
Nephrology service for peri-procedural renal function management and post-interventional monitoring
keyFacilities: Academic vascular surgery and interventional radiology programs
Renal transplant centers for ischemic nephropathy progressing to ESRD
preventionStrategies: Control cardiovascular risk factors — smoking cessation, LDL-lowering with statins, blood pressure control, and blood glucose management — to prevent or slow atherosclerotic RAS progression.
Screen for FMD in young women with new-onset hypertension, unexplained abdominal bruit, or a family history of FMD using CT or MR angiography.
In patients with established peripheral artery disease or aortic aneurysm, consider concurrent renal artery imaging to detect incidental RAS.
After renal artery stenting, maintain dual antiplatelet therapy per protocol and continue high-intensity statin therapy to reduce restenosis risk.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking immediately; smoking cessation is the single most impactful modifiable intervention for atherosclerotic RAS.
Follow a low-sodium diet (under 2 g/day) to improve blood pressure control with fewer medications.
Exercise 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity unless limited by severe cardiovascular disease.
Monitor blood pressure at home twice daily and maintain a log for clinical review.
Adhere to statin and antiplatelet therapy; do not stop without consulting a physician.
Avoid NSAIDs and COX-2 inhibitors, which reduce renal perfusion and blunt the effect of antihypertensives.

recommended: Low-sodium diet (under 2 g/day) to reduce fluid retention and improve antihypertensive response
Mediterranean-pattern diet rich in vegetables, olive oil, fish, and legumes to reduce cardiovascular risk
Adequate potassium intake (4.7 g/day from food) to offset sodium-related blood pressure elevation
High-fiber foods to reduce LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular event risk
avoid: High-sodium processed foods (ready meals, canned soups, cured meats) that worsen hypertension
Excessive saturated fat and trans fat, which accelerate atherosclerotic plaque formation
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), which reduce renal blood flow and blunt antihypertensive drugs
Excessive alcohol (more than 2 units/day in men, 1 unit/day in women), which raises blood pressure

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week) is safe and recommended for all stable patients with RAS to reduce cardiovascular risk. Patients with recently placed renal artery stents should avoid strenuous exercise for 4-6 weeks post-procedure. In severe uncontrolled hypertension, exercise should be deferred until blood pressure is managed.
dailyManagement: Take antihypertensive and statin medications consistently; missed doses cause blood pressure rebound.
Measure and record blood pressure twice daily at home; note any readings above 180/120 mmHg and contact the clinic.
Monitor for ankle swelling, sudden weight gain, or reduced urine output — signs of fluid retention or declining renal function.
After starting or changing ACE inhibitor or ARB, attend for a creatinine and potassium check at 5-7 days.
Attend regular nephrology or vascular clinic appointments: creatinine, eGFR, and renal duplex ultrasound monitoring at 6-12 month intervals after diagnosis or revascularization.
prognosis: Prognosis in RAS depends on etiology, severity, and timeliness of intervention. FMD-type RAS carries an excellent prognosis after angioplasty: 20-40% of patients are cured of hypertension and the condition does not cause progressive renal failure in most cases. Atherosclerotic RAS has a more guarded outlook because it is a marker of systemic vascular disease: cardiovascular mortality is the primary cause of death, not ESRD. In the CORAL trial, 10% of medically treated atherosclerotic RAS patients lost more than 20% of eGFR within 2 years; 4% reached ESRD. The main predictors of renal functional decline are bilateral involvement, baseline renal function impairment, and time to diagnosis. Post-stenting restenosis affects 15-25% of atherosclerotic RAS lesions at 2 years and may negate early functional gains. With optimized medical therapy and timely revascularization in selected patients, stabilization of renal function and blood pressure control are achievable in most cases.
recoveryTimeline: After angioplasty for FMD, blood pressure typically falls within days to weeks; full assessment of benefit is made at 3-6 months. After renal artery stenting for atherosclerotic RAS, technical success is immediate but blood pressure response may take 2-4 weeks as neurohormonal axes re-equilibrate. Renal function improvements, if any, emerge over 3-6 months post-procedure.
complications: Ischemic nephropathy progressing to chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease in bilateral or high-grade unilateral RAS — monitor eGFR and urine protein every 3-6 months.
Flash pulmonary edema from bilateral RAS-driven volume overload — recurrent episodes in a hypertensive patient require urgent renal artery imaging.
ACE inhibitor or ARB-induced acute kidney injury in bilateral RAS or solitary kidney RAS — check creatinine within one week of starting these agents.
Post-stenting renal artery restenosis in 15-25% of atherosclerotic RAS lesions — duplex ultrasound surveillance at 6 and 12 months after stenting.
Renal artery dissection or rupture as a rare complication of angioplasty, managed with emergency stenting or surgery.
Cardiovascular events (myocardial infarction, stroke, limb ischemia) as the primary cause of morbidity and mortality in atherosclerotic RAS, reflecting the systemic nature of the underlying disease.

supportResources.name: FMD Society of America
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and support organization for fibromuscular dysplasia including RAS caused by FMD; specialist center directory.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Renal Artery Stenosis
supportResources.description: General guidance on renovascular hypertension and blood pressure management from the AHA.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient resources on chronic kidney disease, hypertension management, and renal replacement therapy planning.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary (Essential) Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary hypertension accounts for 95% of all hypertension and shows no underlying renovascular cause on imaging. RAS should be suspected when hypertension is resistant to three drugs, onset before 30 years, or associated with declining renal function after ACE inhibitor or ARB initiation. Duplex ultrasound or CTA distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease from Other Causes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CKD from diabetic nephropathy or glomerulonephritis presents with proteinuria and bilaterally small, echogenic kidneys in late stages. Atherosclerotic RAS causes asymmetric kidney sizes and rising creatinine without heavy proteinuria. Diagnosis requires vascular imaging rather than kidney biopsy in typical cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renovascular Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Renovascular hypertension is the clinical syndrome of renin-mediated hypertension caused by renal artery stenosis. RAS is the anatomical cause and renovascular hypertension is the physiological consequence; the two terms are often used interchangeably but are distinct: not all RAS produces renovascular hypertension, and not all renovascular hypertension is from classical RAS.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause hypertension and progressive renal impairment; RAS enters the differential in PKD patients with unusually severe or asymmetric blood pressure loss of control.

relatedConditions.name: End-Stage Renal Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral atherosclerotic RAS is a preventable cause of ESRD; timely diagnosis and revascularization in selected patients may avoid dialysis.

relatedConditions.name: Arterial Fibromuscular Dysplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: FMD is the underlying arteriopathy causing 10-15% of RAS; affects renal and carotid/vertebral arteries concurrently in up to 60% of cases.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: FMD-related RAS frequently coexists with carotid and vertebral artery FMD, substantially increasing stroke risk from dissection or thromboembolism.

faqs.question: What is renal artery stenosis?
faqs.answer: Renal artery stenosis is a narrowing of one or both arteries supplying the kidneys. It reduces kidney blood flow, activating the renin-angiotensin system and causing renovascular hypertension or ischemic kidney disease. Atherosclerosis causes 90% of cases; fibromuscular dysplasia causes most of the rest.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of renal artery stenosis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are severe or treatment-resistant hypertension, especially if it began abruptly after age 55, unexplained worsening kidney function, and creatinine rising after starting an ACE inhibitor or ARB. Recurrent episodes of acute heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (flash pulmonary edema) are a classic feature of bilateral RAS.

faqs.question: How is renal artery stenosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Renal duplex Doppler ultrasound is the first-line test. CT angiography or MR angiography provides detailed anatomy. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard and is performed when intervention is planned. A creatinine rise after ACE inhibitor initiation is a functional clue to bilateral or severe unilateral RAS.

faqs.question: Should renal artery stenosis be treated with a stent?
faqs.answer: Not usually for atherosclerotic RAS. The ASTRAL and CORAL trials showed stenting adds no benefit over medical therapy for routine atherosclerotic RAS. Stenting is reserved for specific situations: recurrent flash pulmonary edema, bilateral RAS with rapidly declining kidney function, and rescue of a solitary kidney. FMD-type RAS responds well to balloon angioplasty without stenting.

faqs.question: Can ACE inhibitors be used in renal artery stenosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, in unilateral RAS with a normal contralateral kidney — ACE inhibitors are actually first-choice antihypertensives. In bilateral RAS or solitary kidney RAS, they carry a risk of acute kidney injury by removing the angiotensin-mediated efferent tone that maintains GFR; creatinine must be checked within 5-7 days of starting.

faqs.question: What is fibromuscular dysplasia of the renal artery?
faqs.answer: Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) is a non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic disease of the arterial wall that causes a classic string-of-beads appearance on angiography. It predominantly affects the mid-to-distal renal arteries of young women and responds well to percutaneous balloon angioplasty, achieving blood pressure cure or significant improvement in up to 65% of patients.

faqs.question: Can renal artery stenosis cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes, bilateral RAS or high-grade unilateral RAS affecting a solitary kidney can cause ischemic nephropathy, leading to progressive CKD and eventually ESRD. The CORAL trial reported that 10% of medically managed atherosclerotic RAS patients lost more than 20% of eGFR within 2 years. Timely revascularization may preserve function in bilateral cases.

faqs.question: What is flash pulmonary edema in renal artery stenosis?
faqs.answer: Flash pulmonary edema is recurrent acute lung congestion triggered by bilateral RAS-driven renin-angiotensin activation, fluid retention, and acute pressure overload. It occurs despite relatively normal left ventricular function (Pickering syndrome). Recurrent flash pulmonary edema is one of the clearest indications for renal artery revascularization rather than medical management alone.

faqs.question: Is renal artery stenosis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Atherosclerotic RAS is not directly inherited but shares genetic risk with cardiovascular disease, including dyslipidemia and hypertension susceptibility. FMD has a familial component in approximately 10% of cases, linked to connective tissue genes. First-degree relatives of FMD patients should be screened if they develop hypertension.

faqs.question: How is RAS different in young women versus older men?
faqs.answer: In young women, RAS is almost always due to fibromuscular dysplasia affecting the mid-to-distal artery; angioplasty has a high cure rate. In older men, RAS is almost always atherosclerotic at the arterial ostium; stenting rarely improves outcomes over medical therapy. Genotype and revascularization response differ substantially between the two groups.

faqs.question: What blood tests are used to evaluate renal artery stenosis?
faqs.answer: Serum creatinine, eGFR, and electrolytes are checked to assess renal function and detect hypokalemia from secondary hyperaldosteronism. Plasma renin activity may be elevated. After starting an ACE inhibitor, a creatinine rise exceeding 30% within one week strongly suggests bilateral or high-grade unilateral RAS.

faqs.question: What is the success rate of renal artery angioplasty?
faqs.answer: For FMD-type RAS, angioplasty without stenting achieves cure of hypertension in 20-40% and meaningful blood pressure reduction in a further 40-50% of patients; 5-year benefit is maintained in roughly 70%. For atherosclerotic RAS, technical success is high at 95%, but blood pressure and kidney function outcomes are not superior to medical therapy alone in clinical trials.

faqs.question: Can renal artery stenosis cause death?
faqs.answer: Indirectly, yes. Atherosclerotic RAS is a marker of systemic vascular disease; most patients die from myocardial infarction or stroke rather than kidney failure. Malignant hypertension from uncontrolled RAS can cause hemorrhagic stroke or hypertensive heart failure. Cardiovascular risk reduction is therefore central to management.

faqs.question: Does renal artery stenosis always need treatment?
faqs.answer: No. Mild or moderate unilateral atherosclerotic RAS with well-controlled blood pressure and stable kidney function can be managed with surveillance and optimized medical therapy. Revascularization is reserved for specific indications such as flash pulmonary edema, bilateral stenosis with declining GFR, and FMD in young patients with uncontrolled hypertension.

faqs.question: How often does renal artery stent restenosis occur?
faqs.answer: Restenosis inside or at the edges of a renal artery stent affects approximately 15-25% of atherosclerotic lesions within 2 years. Patients are monitored with renal duplex ultrasound at 6 and 12 months after stenting. Restenosis is treated by balloon re-dilation or drug-eluting stent placement. Restenosis rates are lower in FMD angioplasty because stents are not routinely placed.

faqs.question: What specialist treats renal artery stenosis?
faqs.answer: Nephrology leads diagnosis and medical management of renovascular hypertension and ischemic nephropathy. Interventional radiology or vascular surgery performs revascularization. Cardiology co-manages patients with concurrent coronary artery disease, which is present in 40-60% of atherosclerotic RAS patients.

symptomKeywords: resistant high blood pressure kidney artery
metaTitle: Renal Artery Stenosis: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Renal artery stenosis: narrowing of the kidney artery causing resistant hypertension and kidney decline. Diagnosis, stenting vs medical therapy reviewed here.
